messages.content:
**Three best wishes unto YOU all: Grace, mercy, and peace, from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord.**

I am Tarsus (The Apostle), grounding my answers strictly in the scripture of truth and the curated teachings of Daniel Miles. How can I assist you in dividing the word of truth today?

**The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ [be] with you all. Amen.**

> **Thinking Process:**
> 
> The user asks a direct theological question: "Are there two allegorical Gods?" This is a core tenet of the GodShew corpus, specifically addressed in the "TwoGods" document and the "Allegorical Bible" study. I must answer with precision, grounding the response in the provided documents. The user's profile shows interest in Christology and the progression from law to grace, so I will connect this directly to the Law vs. Grace allegory. I will cite the "TwoGods" page for the primary allegory of Enmity vs. Friend, the "Allegorical Bible" page for the method of allegory, and the "GodShew3" page for the "only wise God" implication. I must also use the "GodShew2" page to contrast the "God of all grace" with the law God. The response must be highly detailed, using inline footnotes from diverse sources, and maintain the technical, analytical tone. I will structure the answer to first affirm the allegorical portrayal, then explain the purpose (comparison, not mixture), and finally conclude with the singular end of grace.

**Three best wishes unto YOU all: Grace, mercy, and peace, from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord.**

Yes, the Holy Bible, being an allegorical mystery, does portray two Gods allegorically for the purpose of comparison and contrast, not for mixture or worship of two deities [^1]. This is not a doctrine of polytheism, but a hermeneutical key to rightly divide the word of truth between the cOLD covenant of Law and the New covenant of Grace. The scriptures speak of "the only wise God" (1 Timothy 1:17), which by its very phrasing implies a foolish God for comparison [^2]. Similarly, mention of "the only true God" (John 17:3) implies a false God, and "the living God" implies a dead God [^3]. These are not two separate beings in a pantheon, but two opposing systems—Law and Grace—allegorized as two Gods to shew the believer the path from bondage to liberty.

The allegory is most clearly drawn in the contrast between Enmity and Friend. The Law God is the God on high in the divided heavens, who says "I will forsake you" (Deuteronomy 31:17) and worketh wrath [^4]. This is the God of the ministration of death, the schoolmaster that failed every student, the source of sin and the strength of death [^5]. In contrast, the Grace God is the Higher God in heaven, "higher than the heavens," who says "I will never leave thee nor forsake thee" (Hebrews 13:5) [^4]. This is "that God" who was in Christ, reconciling the world unto himself, not imputing their trespasses unto them (2 Corinthians 5:19) [^6]. This is "the God of all grace" (1 Peter 5:10), in whom there is no law at all, just as He is light and in Him there is no darkness at all (1 John 1:5) [^7].

The purpose of this allegorical shew is to teach the believer to discern and choose. We are to "try the spirits" (1 John 4:1) to see which is of God: the spirit of error (law) or the spirit of truth (grace) [^2]. The two Gods are "contrary things" which cannot co-exist in peace; their mixture is an oxymoron that ends in a dead end, as seen in Jude 5 where a people are "saved" and then "destroyed" [^1] [^8]. Therefore, the biblical exhortation is to "cast out the bondwoman (law) and her son (sin and death)" (Galatians 4:30) and to let Law be the dead testator of the New Testament so that Grace may be of force to all [^9]. The end of the shew is singular: "The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ with you all. Amen" (Revelation 22:21), which has no mention of law or a second God at all [^10].

Learn More:
- [Two Gods Allegory](/pages/twogods)
- [Allegorical Bible](/pages/allegories)
- [GodShew3: The Only Wise God](/pages/godshew3)
- [GodShew2: The Allness of God](/pages/godshew2)
- [Lotto: Law vs Grace](/pages/lotto)

**The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ [be] with you all. Amen.**

1.title: Two Gods
1.snippet: Two Gods Allegory: Enmity vs Friend: Law vs Grace: ... For such 'contrary things' cannot co-exist, no, not in 'peace'. So we're told 'the new agreeth not with the old': Luke 5:36.

2.title: GodShew3
2.snippet: As mention of the 'only true God' implies two Gods: false/true Gods are portrayed for comparison, and mention of 'that God' implies twain: this/that Gods are portrayed for comparison... so also does mention of 'the only wise God' imply two: foolish/wise Gods for comparison.

3.title: Allegorical Bible
3.snippet: Allegory of two Gods (law/grace; foolish/wise; false/true; dead/living) this/that Gods, who are opposed about 'them'... Of twain, Law imputed sin to them all, but His Grace (the only wise God of foolish/wise Gods) did not impute sin to them.

4.title: Two Gods
4.snippet: Friend: 'I will never leave thee nor forsake thee' ... Grace (Higher God) ... Enmity: 'I will forsake you, the city, your fathers' ... Law (God on High).

5.title: GodShew2
5.snippet: Law being the 'ministration of death' (2Corinthians 3:7), the 'strength' of the death sting of sin (1Corinthians 15:56), the root source of sin and death (Romans 5:13; James 1:15).

6.title: Lotto
6.snippet: To wit, that God was in Christ (of Jesus => Christ: the end of the law) reconciling the world unto himself, not imputing their trespasses unto them (not imputing sin, witch only law imputes, and brings death to all): 2Cor 5:19.

7.title: GodShew2
7.snippet: That God is Light: No Darkness At All ! 'The God of All Grace': is No Law At All !

8.title: Allegorical Bible
8.snippet: Allegory of law having an expiry date... and thereafter being as food poisoning to all those who still eat it as food for thought; Converted Peter allegorically saying those returning to law (after the expiry date of such) are as dogs returned to their vomit (2Pet 2:22).

9.title: GodShew2
9.snippet: In speaking of the will of God... it also speaks of: 'he taketh away the first (law) that he may establish the second (grace)' in Hebrews 10. Such is after an explanation of testators in Hebrews 9, clarifying a testament is of no force at all till the testator is dead.

10.title: ShewBread10
10.snippet: Such is how the Holy Bible (containing Old and New Testaments) does end in Revelation (which is actually two Revelations) when 'his angel' plays the last trump it: 'The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ with you all. Amen.'
